q:
Management Training Course
A large manufacturing company has tens of thousands of employees, and 30 percent of the employees have completed a management training course. Suppose a simple random sample of 50 employees is selected.Which of the following is closest to the probability that more than half of the sample will be employees who have completed the management training course?

Late to Class
Attendance records at a large high school show that 6 percent of students are late to school on a typical day. The students in an AP Statistics class believe the rate is higher than 6 percent. The students took a simple random sample of 75 students from the school and asked, “Were you late to school today?” In their sample, 6 out of the 75 students, or 8 percent of the sample, said they were late to school that day. The AP Statistics students conducted a simulation with 100 samples of 75 students from a population where 6 percent of students are late. The results of the simulation are shown in the dotplot. Based on the simulation and the results of the AP Statistics class’s sample, is there convincing statistical evidence that more than 6 percent of all students at the school are late to school on a typical day?
o: Yes, because 8% of students in their sample said they were late to school that day.
Yes, because about 47% of the simulated sample percentages of students late to school are 6% or more.
No, because about 47% of the simulated sample percentages of students late to school are 6% or more.
Yes, because about 29% of the simulated sample percentages of students late to school are 8% or more.
No, because about 29% of the simulated sample percentages of students late to school are 8% or more.

q:
Describe sampling distribution of eligible voters
According to data from the United States Elections Project, only 36 percent of eligible voters voted in the 2014 elections. For random samples of size 40, which of the following best describes the sampling distribution of p^, the sample proportion of people who voted in the 2014 elections?
o: The sampling distribution is skewed to the left, with mean 0.36 and standard deviation 0.076 .
The sampling distribution is skewed to the right, with mean 0.64 and standard deviation 0.006 .
The sampling distribution is approximately normal, with mean 0.36 and standard deviation 0.076 .
The sampling distribution is approximately normal, with mean 0.36 and standard deviation 0.006 .
The sampling distribution is approximately normal, with mean 0.64 and standard deviation 0.076 .

q:
Describe sampling distribution for mean wait time
At a certain restaurant, the distribution of wait times between ordering a meal and receiving the meal has mean 11.4 minutes and standard deviation 2.6 minutes. The restaurant manager wants to find the probability that the mean wait time will be greater than 12.0 minutes for a random sample of 84 customers. Assuming the wait times among customers are independent, which of the following describes the sampling distribution of the sample mean wait time for random samples of size 84 ?
o: Approximately normal with mean 11.4 minutes and standard deviation 2.6 minutes
Approximately normal with mean 11.4 minutes and standard deviation the fraction 2.6 over the square root of 84 minute
Approximately normal with mean 12.0 minutes and standard deviation 2.6 minutes
Binomial with mean 84 times 0.41 minutes and standard deviation the square root of 84 times 0.41, times 0.59, end root minutes
Binomial with mean 84 times 0.5 minutes and standard deviation the square root of 84 times 0.5, times 0.5, end root minutes

q:
Effect of reducing sample size for online purchase
The director of a marketing department wants to estimate the proportion of people who purchase a certain product online. The director originally planned to obtain a random sample of 2 , 500 people who purchased the product. However, because of budget concerns, the sample size will be reduced to 1 , 500 people. Which of the following describes the effect of reducing the number of people in the sample?
o: The variance of the sample will increase.
The variance of the population will decrease.
The variance of the sampling distribution of the estimator will increase.
The variance of the sampling distribution of the estimator will decrease.
The variance of the sampling distribution of the estimator will remain the same.

q:
Senator age
In a recent year, the distribution of age for senators in the United States Senate was unimodal and roughly symmetric with mean 65 years and standard deviation 10.6 years. Consider a simulation with 200 trials in which, for each trial, a random sample of 5 senators’ ages is selected and the mean age calculated.Which of the following best describes the distribution of the 200 sample mean ages?
o: Approximately normal with mean 65 years and standard deviation 10.6 years
Approximately normal with mean 65 years and standard deviation 10.6 over the square root of 5 years
Approximately normal with mean 65 years and standard deviation 10.6 over the square root of 200 years
Approximately uniform with mean 65 years and standard deviation 10.6 over the square root of 5 years
Approximately uniform with mean 65 years and standard deviation 10.6 over the square root of 200 years

q:
Probability of sample mean dexterity task time
A recent study was conducted to investigate the duration of time required to complete a certain manual dexterity task. The reported mean was 10.2 seconds with a standard deviation of 16.0 seconds. Suppose the reported values are the true mean and standard deviation for the population of subjects in the study. If a random sample of 144 subjects is selected from the population, what is the approximate probability that the mean of the sample will be more than 11.0 seconds?
o: Based on the values of the true mean and true standard deviation, it can be concluded that the population distribution is not normal and therefore the probability cannot be calculated.

q:
Describe sampling distribution for mean home price
There were 5,317 previously owned homes sold in a western city in the year 2000. The distribution of the sales prices of these homes was strongly right-skewed, with a mean of $206,274 and a standard deviation of $37,881. If all possible simple random samples of size 100 are drawn from this population and the mean is computed for each of these samples, which of the following describes the sampling distribution of the sample mean?
o: Approximately normal with mean $206,274 and standard deviation $3,788
Approximately normal with mean $206,274 and standard deviation $37,881
Approximately normal with mean $206,274 and standard deviation $520
Strongly right-skewed with mean $206,274 and standard deviation $3,788
Strongly right-skewed with mean $206,274 and standard deviation $37,881

q:
Sampling variability in birth sex percentage
Approximately 52 percent of all recent births were boys. In a simple random sample of 100 recent births, 49 were boys and 51 were girls. The most likely explanation for the difference between the observed results and the expected results in this case is
o: bias
variability due to sampling
nonsampling error
a sampling frame that is incomplete
confounding

q:
Choose n and p for greatest standard deviation
Which of the following pairs of sample size n and population proportion p would produce the greatest standard deviation for the sampling distribution of a sample proportion p̂?
o: n = 1,000 and p close to 0
n = 1,000 and p close to 1
n = 1,000 and p close to 1/2
n = 100 and p close to 0
n = 100 and p close to 1/2

q:
Variability of mean TV hours as point estimator
Researchers will conduct a study of the television-viewing habits of children. They will select a simple random sample of children and record the number of hours of television the children watch per week. The researchers will report the sample mean as a point estimate for the population mean. Which of the following statements is correct for the sample mean as a point estimator?
o: A sample of size 25 will produce more variability of the estimator than a sample of size 50.
A sample of size 25 will produce less variability of the estimator than a sample of size 50.
A sample of size 25 will produce a biased estimator, but a sample size of 50 will produce an unbiased estimator.
A sample of size 25 will produce a more biased estimator than a sample of size 50.
A sample of size 25 will produce a less biased estimator than a sample of size 50.

The manager of a symphony in a large city wants to investigate music preferences for adults and students in the city. Let pA represent the population proportion of adults who live in the city who prefer pop music. Let pS represent the population proportion of students who live in the city who prefer pop music. Random samples of 200 adults from the city and 200 students from the city will be selected.Which of the following is the best interpretation of P(p^A−p^S>0)≈0.022 ?
o: The difference between the population proportion of adults who prefer pop music and the population proportion of students who prefer pop music is approximately 0.022.
The difference between the population proportion of adults who prefer pop music and the population proportion of students who prefer pop music is 0.

For all random samples of 200 adults from the city and 200 students from the city, the difference between the sample proportion of adults who prefer pop music and the sample proportion of students who prefer pop music will be 0.022.

For all random samples of 200 adults from the city and 200 students from the city, the difference between the sample proportion of adults who prefer pop music and the sample proportion of students who prefer pop music will be 0.

For all random samples of 200 adults from the city and 200 students from the city, the sample proportion of adults who prefer pop music will be greater than the sample proportion of students who prefer pop music in about 2.2% of samples.

q:
Popcorn
A consumer group is investigating two brands of popcorn, R and S. The population proportion of kernels that will pop for Brand R is 0.90. The population proportion of kernels that will pop for Brand S is 0.85. Two independent random samples were taken from the population. The following table shows the sample statistics. Number of Kernels in Samples Proportion from Sample that Popped Brand R 100 0.92 Brand S 200 0.89 The consumer group claims that for all samples of size 100 kernels from Brand R and 200 kernels from Brand S, the mean of all possible differences in sample proportions (Brand R minus Brand S) is 0.03. Is the consumer group’s claim correct?
o: Yes. The mean is 0.92 minus 0.89 equals 0.03 .
No. The mean is the fraction 0.92 plus 0.89 over 2 equals 0.905 .
No. The mean is the fraction 0.92 minus 0.89 over 2 equals 0.015 .
No. The mean is the fraction 0.90 plus 0.85 over 2 equals 0.875 .
No. The mean is 0.90 minus 0.85 equals 0.05 .

A random sample of 16 students will be selected and the mean number of pets for the sample will be calculated.What is the mean of the sampling distribution of the sample mean for samples of size 16 ?
o: the fraction 3.2 over the square root of 16
the fraction 1.7 over the square root of 16

Mean 4.9 letters and standard deviation 0.15 letter
Mean 4.9 letters and standard deviation 1.5 letters
Mean 0.49 letter and standard deviation 0.15 letter

For each trial, the die is tossed 5 times, and the mean of the 5 values landing face up is recorded.The mean and standard deviation of the results of the simulation should be close to which of the following?

Mean 3.5 and standard deviation 0.7638

q:
Two samples of age
The distribution of age for players of a certain professional sport is strongly skewed to the right with mean 26.8 years and standard deviation 4.2 years. Consider a random sample of 4 players and a different random sample of 50 players from the population.Which of the following statements is true about the sampling distributions of the sample mean ages for samples of size 4 and samples of size 50 ?
o: Both will be skewed to the right, and the mean for size 50 will be closer to 26.8 than the mean for size 4.
Both will be skewed to the right, and the standard deviation for size 50 will be closer to 4.2 than the standard deviation for size 4.
Both will be approximately normal, and the mean for size 50 will be closer to 26.8 than the mean for size 4.
Only the sampling distribution for size 4 will be approximately normal, and the standard deviation for both will be 4.2.
Only the sampling distribution for size 50 will be approximately normal, and the mean for both will be 26.8.

o: The probability that each of the 5 backpacks selected will have a weight above 22 pounds is approximately 0.05.
The probability that each of the 5 backpacks selected will have a weight above 19.7 pounds is approximately 0.05.
The probability that the population mean is greater than 22 pounds is approximately 0.05.
For all samples of size 5, approximately 5% of the sample will have a probability greater than 22 pounds.

q:

Let x¯1 represent the average trunk diameter of four randomly sampled giant sequoia trees, and let x¯2 represent the average trunk diameter of three randomly sampled California redwood trees. If the random sampling is done with replacement, what is the standard deviation σ(x¯1−x¯2) of the sampling distribution of the difference in sample means x¯1−x¯2 ?

q:
Track team
A large school district held a district-wide track meet for all high school students. For the 2-mile run, the population of female students participating had a mean running time of 8.8 minutes with standard deviation of 3.3 minutes, and the population of male students participating had a mean running time 7.3 minutes with standard deviation of 2.9 minutes. Suppose 8 female students and 8 male students who participated in the 2-mile run are selected at random from each population. Let x¯F represent the sample mean running time for the female students, and let x¯M represent the sample mean running time for the male students.What are the mean and standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the difference in sample means x¯F−x¯M ?
o: The mean is 0.4, and the standard deviation is the square root of the fraction 8.8 over 8, end fraction, plus the fraction 7.3 over 8, end root .
The mean is 0.4, and the standard deviation is the square root of the fraction 8.8 squared over 8, end fraction, plus the fraction 7.3 squared over 8, end root .
The mean is 1.5, and the standard deviation is the square root of the fraction 3.3 squared over 8, end fraction, minus the fraction 2.9 squared over 8, end root .
The mean is 1.5, and the standard deviation is the square root of the fraction 3.3 over 8, end fraction, plus the fraction 2.9 over 8, end root .
The mean is 1.5, and the standard deviation is the square root of the fraction 3.3 squared over 8, end fraction, plus the fraction 2.9 squared over 8, end root .

q:
Two bats
Cheryl practices hitting a softball in an indoor stadium by using both an aluminum bat and a composite bat made of carbon fiber and graphite. She records the distance traveled by the ball for each hit. Let x¯1 represent the average distance traveled by balls hit with the aluminum bat, and let x¯2 represent the average distance traveled by balls hit with the composite bat. Assume Cheryl’s batting practice hits are independent.Which of the following conditions are sufficient to model the sampling distribution of x¯1−x¯2 with a normal distribution?There are at least 30 recorded distances traveled for each type of bat.The distribution of distance traveled by the ball is approximately normal for each bat.The total number of distances traveled is at least 60 for the two bats combined.
o: 1 only
2 only
3 only
1 and 2 only
2 and 3 only

q:
Bio and business majors
At a large university, the division of computing services surveyed a random sample of 45 biology majors and 55 business majors from populations of over 1,000 biology and 1,000 business majors. The sampled students were asked how many hours they spend per week using any university computer lab. Let x¯1 represent the average hours per week spent in any university computer lab by the 45 biology majors, and let x¯2 represent the average hours per week spent in any university computer lab by the 55 business majors.Which of the following is the best explanation for why the sampling distribution of x¯1−x¯2 can be modeled with a normal distribution?
o: The two sample standard deviations are assumed to be equal.
The sample sizes are both sufficiently large.
The distributions of the population are normal.
The population standard deviations are assumed equal.
There are at least 30 students in each of the two populations.

In a random sample of 41 horses from the second population, the mean age of the sample was 19 years.Is the sampling distribution of the difference in sample mean ages approximately normal?
o: Yes, because the two populations of wild horses can be modeled by a normal distribution.
Yes, because the samples were selected at random.
Yes, because the sample sizes are both greater than 30.
No, because the populations are not normal.
No, because the difference in sample mean ages was not 0.

q:

For a population of Sussex chickens, the mean number of eggs laid per chicken is 0.50 with standard deviation 0.10 egg. Two independent random samples of chickens were taken from the populations. The following table shows the sample statistics. n x¯ s Leghorn 36 0.75 0.30 Sussex 36 0.45 0.12 Mike claims that for all samples of size 36 from the population of leghorn chickens and all samples of size 36 from the population of Sussex chickens, the mean of all possible differences in sample means (leghorn minus Sussex) is 0.30 eggs per chicken. Is Mike’s claim correct?
o: Yes. The mean is 0.75 minus 0.45 equals 0.30 egg per chicken.
No. The mean is 0.30 minus 0.12 equals 0.18 egg per chicken.
No. The mean is 0.70 minus 0.50 equals 0.20 egg per chicken.
No. The mean is 0.20 minus 0.10 equals 0.10 egg per chicken.
No. The mean is 36 times 0.75, minus 36 times 0.45, equals 10.8 eggs per chicken.

q:
Wave height
The distribution of ocean wave height at a certain California beach is approximately normal with mean 7.2 feet. The distribution of ocean wave height at a certain Florida beach is approximately normal with mean 6.6 feet. Six waves from each beach will be selected at random and the heights will be recorded. Let x¯C represent the sample mean height of the 6 California waves, and let x¯F represent the sample mean height of the 6 Florida waves.Which of the following is the best interpretation of P(x¯C−x¯F>0.5)=0.55 ?
o: The probability that the heights for all 6 California waves will exceed the heights for all 6 Florida waves by more than 0.55 feet is 0.5.
The probability that the heights for all 6 California waves will exceed the heights for all 6 Florida waves by more than 0.5 feet is 0.55.
The probability of observing a difference (California minus Florida) greater than 0.5 feet between the mean height of 6 California waves and the mean height of 6 Florida waves is 0.55.
The probability of observing a difference greater than 0.5 feet between the height of one wave in California and the height of one wave in Florida is 0.55.
The probability of observing a difference greater than 0.55 feet between the height of one wave in California and the height of one wave in Florida is 0.5.

q:
Election Voting
For the population of people in the United States who are at least 18 years of age, approximately 61 percent voted in a recent election. A random sample of 200 people and a random sample of 100 people will be selected from the population.For which sample size is it more likely that greater than 70 percent of the sample voted in the recent election?
o: They are equally likely, because the mean of the sampling distribution is the same for both sample sizes.
It is more likely for a sample size of 200 people, because there is more variability in the sampling distribution for larger sample sizes.
It is more likely for a sample size of 200 people, because there is less variability in the sampling distribution for larger sample sizes.
It is more likely for a sample of size 100 people, because there is more variability in the sampling distribution for smaller sample sizes.
It is more likely for a sample size of 100 people, because there is less variability in the sampling distribution for smaller sample sizes.
